name: Lakshmidhara
dates_notes: Conventionally dated to the sixteenth century on the basis of his position in the Śrīvidyā commentarial chain between Amṛtānandanātha (fourteenth century) and Bhāskararāya Makhin (1690–1785). The *Lakṣmīdharī* on the *Saundarya-Laharī* is the canonical sixteenth-century Śrīvidyā exegesis; the precise dates are uncertain but the sixteenth-century placement is accepted by Brooks (*The Secret of the Three Cities*, 1990) and by Subramanian's standard editions.
core_thesis: Lakṣmīdhara is the principal sixteenth-century Śrīvidyā commentator whose *Lakṣmīdharī* commentary on the *Saundarya-Laharī* — the great Śrīvidyā devotional hymn attributed to Śaṅkara — fixes the *samaya*-line interpretation of the school. The *Saundarya-Laharī* (in two parts: the *Ānanda-Laharī* and the *Saundarya-Laharī* proper, totalling 100 verses on traditional reckoning) is the principal Sanskrit devotional articulation of the Śrīvidyā worship of Lalitā-Tripurasundarī and the *Śrī-Cakra*; the commentarial tradition on the hymn becomes the principal medieval Śrīvidyā theological literature. Lakṣmīdhara's commentary is distinctive on two counts. First, he reads the *Saundarya-Laharī* through the *samaya* ("internal") line of Śrīvidyā as opposed to the *kaula* ("external", with antinomian ritual) line, treating the hymn as a meditative-recognitional articulation of the *Śrī-Cakra* as the internal structure of the *sādhaka*'s own consciousness rather than as a programme of external transgressive ritual. The *samaya / kaula* distinction is the principal sectarian division within Śrīvidyā, and Lakṣmīdhara is the principal early-modern *samaya* commentator. Second, his commentary is the principal medieval source through which the *Saundarya-Laharī*'s mantra-and-cakra cosmology is articulated within the broader non-dual Śaiva-Śākta framework of the Amṛtānanda line. The work is the immediate predecessor of Bhāskararāya's mature Śrīvidyā commentarial project and is the canonical text from which the modern South-Indian Śrīvidyā tradition derives its meditative reading of the *Saundarya-Laharī*.

engaged_works.title: Lakshmidhari (commentary on Saundarya-Lahari)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Lakṣmīdhara; the standard sixteenth-century commentary on the *Saundarya-Laharī*. Edited in the standard Sanskrit editions (Subramanian; Anantakrishna Sastri; cf. the Adyar Library and Research Centre publications).

ascription_evidence.description: Brooks 1990 (*The Secret of the Three Cities*); the standard Sanskrit editions; Subramanian's translation.

engaged_works.summary: Verse-by-verse commentary on the *Saundarya-Laharī*'s 100 verses, articulating the *samaya*-line interpretation of Śrīvidyā. The opening verses of the hymn (1–41, the *Ānanda-Laharī* portion) treat the metaphysics of *śakti* as the active principle without which Śiva is inert ("Śiva united with Śakti is able to create — without her, he cannot stir" — *Saundarya-Laharī* 1); the *Śrī-Cakra*'s nine-fold structural articulation; the goddess's *mantra*-cosmology (the fifteen-syllable *Śrī-Vidyā*, the sixteen-syllable *ṣoḍaśī*); and the *kuṇḍalinī*-and-*cakra* yoga (the goddess's residence in the *mūlādhāra* and her ascent through the central channel to the *sahasrāra*). The later verses (42–100, the *Saundarya-Laharī* proper) treat the goddess's bodily form in head-to-foot itemized descriptive worship. Lakṣmīdhara's commentary throughout reads the bodily descriptions as articulations of the *Śrī-Cakra*'s structural levels and of the *sādhaka*'s own internal *cakra*-anatomy, fixing the *samaya* interpretation against the *kaula* alternative. The work is the principal medieval Śrīvidyā devotional commentary and is the immediate source for Bhāskararāya's later Śrīvidyā project.

reception_notes: Lakṣmīdhara is the canonical *samaya*-line Śrīvidyā commentator and the principal sixteenth-century voice in the lineage chain Amṛtānanda → Lakṣmīdhara → Bhāskararāya. The *Saundarya-Laharī* is the most widely read Śrīvidyā devotional text and Lakṣmīdhara's commentary is the principal medieval interpretive vehicle through which it enters the school's living tradition. Modern scholarship (Brooks 1990; Subramanian's editions) establishes the sixteenth-century placement and the *samaya / kaula* distinction as the principal organizing structure within Śrīvidyā that Lakṣmīdhara's commentary fixes.
